title: Get started with coresynth
text: Every estimator reached scm_fit() formula syntax: data must long-format balanced panel (one row per unit–time), treatment 0/1 indicator switches treated units post-treatment periods. method = selects estimator.
code: outcome ~ treatment | unit_id + time_id

title: Get started with coresynth
text: simulate balanced panel 10 units 20 periods. Unit u1 treated period 11 onward true ATT 2.0.
code: set.seed(42) N <- 10; TT <- 20; T_pre <- 10 f   <- cumsum(rnorm(TT, 0, 0.5))     # common factor lam <- rnorm(N, 1, 0.3)              # unit loadings dat <- expand.grid(time = seq_len(TT), id = paste0("u", seq_len(N))) dat$y <- as.vector(outer(f, lam)) + rnorm(nrow(dat), 0, 0.3) dat$d <- as.integer(dat$id == "u1" & dat$time > T_pre) dat$y[dat$d == 1] <- dat$y[dat$d == 1] + 2.0   # inject the treatment effect  head(dat) #>   time id         y d #> 1    1 u1 0.7590559 0 #> 2    2 u1 0.5774968 0 #> 3    3 u1 0.8414384 0 #> 4    4 u1 0.6355518 0 #> 5    5 u1 1.1532557 0 #> 6    6 u1 0.4384856 0

title: Get started with coresynth
text: estimated ATT lives fit$estimate:
code: fit <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "scm") fit #> === coresynth fit === #> Method : SCM  #> Estimate (ATT): 2.2712  #> Pre-treatment periods: 10 fit$estimate #> [1] 2.271164

title: Get started with coresynth
text: interface shared, swapping estimators one-word change. run six data (true ATT = 2.0).
code: methods <- c("scm", "sdid", "gsc", "mc", "tasc", "si") fits    <- lapply(methods, function(m) scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = m)) names(fits) <- methods  data.frame(   method   = methods,   estimate = round(sapply(fits, `[[`, "estimate"), 3) ) #>      method estimate #> scm     scm    2.271 #> sdid   sdid    2.150 #> gsc     gsc    2.255 #> mc       mc    2.696 #> tasc   tasc    1.154 #> si       si    2.346

title: Get started with coresynth
text: plot.coresynth() offers three views via type =.
code: plot(fits$sdid, type = "trend")   # observed vs. synthetic plot(fits$scm, type = "gap")      # treatment effect over time plot(fits$scm, type = "weights")  # donor weights

title: Get started with coresynth
text: coresynth integrates broom, results drop straight tidy workflows paper tables. export_json() writes fit disk JSON reproducibility downstream (e.g. AI) workflows:
code: library(broom)  tidy(fits$scm)     # donor weights as a data frame #>   term   estimate        type #> 1   u2 0.24733877 unit_weight #> 2   u3 0.00000000 unit_weight #> 3   u4 0.07036082 unit_weight #> 4   u5 0.00000000 unit_weight #> 5   u6 0.14754253 unit_weight #> 6   u7 0.32811909 unit_weight #> 7   u8 0.00000000 unit_weight #> 8   u9 0.00000000 unit_weight #> 9  u10 0.20663878 unit_weight glance(fits$scm)   # one-row model summary #>   method estimate n_controls n_treated T_pre T_post staggered multi_arm #> 1    scm 2.271164          9         1    10     10     FALSE     FALSE export_json(fits$scm, file = "scm_result.json")

title: Get started with coresynth
text: Estimators — covariates, predictors, method-specific options six estimators plus experimental-design variant. Inference — placebo, bootstrap, jackknife, parametric, conformal inference. Staggered adoption — cohort-based estimation units adopt treatment different times.

title: Estimators
text: Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller (2010). Donor weights simplex match treated unit’s pre-treatment trajectory.

title: Estimators
text: default (predictors = NULL) pre-treatment outcomes form predictor matrix. match covariates specific outcome lags instead, build list pred(vars, times, op) specs:
code: fit_scm <- scm_fit(   y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "scm",   predictors = list(     pred("income", 1:T_pre),          # mean income over the pre-period     pred("y", T_pre),                  # outcome in the last pre-period     pred("y", 1:4, op = "mean")        # outcome averaged over early pre-period   ) ) fit_scm$estimate #> [1] 2.154071

what: Out-of-sample V selection, donor filtering, penalisation
title: Estimators
code: # Abadie (2021) §3.2: split the pre-period to choose V out of sample fit_oos <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "scm",                    v_selection = "oos")  # Abadie (2021) §4: drop poorly-fitting donors by individual MSPE ratio fit_filt <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "scm",                     donor_mspe_threshold = 5)  # Abadie & L'Hour (2021): penalised SCM (auto-selects the penalty out of sample) fit_pen <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "scm",                    lambda_pen = "auto")  c(oos = fit_oos$estimate, filtered = fit_filt$estimate, penalised = fit_pen$estimate) #>       oos  filtered penalised  #>  2.037281  2.073847  2.073847

title: Estimators
text: augment_scm() applies ridge bias-correction Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein (2021):
code: aug <- augment_scm(fit_scm) c(scm = aug$att_scm, augmented = aug$att_aug) #>       scm augmented  #>  2.154071  2.306569

title: Estimators
text: Arkhangelsky et al. (2021). Combines unit time weights -style double differencing. Time-varying covariates partialled first (Clarke et al. 2023):
code: fit_sdid <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "sdid",                     covariates = "x") fit_sdid$estimate #> [1] 1.975381

title: Estimators
text: Xu (2017). Interactive fixed effects estimated SVD; r sets number latent factors. covariates full EM algorithm used (E-step SVD, M-step ridge OLS), fit$beta holds covariate coefficients. r sensitivity easy sweep:
code: fit_gsc <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "gsc",                    r = 2, covariates = "x") c(estimate = fit_gsc$estimate, beta = unname(fit_gsc$beta)) #>   estimate       beta  #> 1.89883014 0.01877872 sapply(1:3, function(rr)   scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "gsc", r = rr)$estimate) #> [1] 1.912170 1.905871 1.875384

title: Estimators
text: Athey et al. (2021). Nuclear-norm-regularised completion (Soft-Impute). penalty lambda defaults 0.01 * sigma_max(Y); pass number override. small donor pools MC’s nuclear-norm shrinkage often pushes ATT truth — known property (Mazumder et al. 2010), implementation bug.
code: fit_mc_auto <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "mc") fit_mc_man  <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "mc", lambda = 1.0) c(auto = fit_mc_auto$estimate, manual = fit_mc_man$estimate) #>     auto   manual  #> 2.165341 2.165341

title: Estimators
text: Rho et al. (2026). state-space model fitted Kalman EM. r sets latent state dimension, em_iter EM iterations, fix_A = TRUE constrains transition matrix constant.
code: fit_tasc      <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "tasc",                          r = 2, em_iter = 10) fit_tasc_fixA <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "tasc",                          r = 2, em_iter = 10, fix_A = TRUE) c(free_A = fit_tasc$estimate, fixed_A = fit_tasc_fixA$estimate) #>   free_A  fixed_A  #> 2.045796 2.049410

title: Estimators
text: Agarwal et al. (2025). SI-PCR uses truncated SVD donor pre-period. k sets rank (default floor(sqrt(min(T_pre, N_co)))).
code: fit_si <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "si") fit_si$estimate #> [1] 1.925724

title: Estimators
text: SI uniquely supports multiple treatment arms (K > 1). treatment column takes values 0, 1, ..., K, scm_fit() automatically routes multi-arm path: control-arm SVD basis shared across arms (unit factors arm-invariant). Use panel_to_tensor() prepare/inspect tensor structure.
code: set.seed(7) Nc <- 10; T2 <- 16; Tp <- 10 U <- matrix(rnorm(T2 * 2), T2, 2) V <- matrix(rnorm((Nc + 4) * 2), Nc + 4, 2) Lam <- rbind(c(1, 1), c(1.4, .6), c(.6, 1.4))   # arm 0, 1, 2 arm_unit <- c(rep(0L, Nc), 1L, 1L, 2L, 2L) g <- expand.grid(time = seq_len(T2), id = seq_len(Nc + 4)) g$arm <- arm_unit[g$id] g$d   <- ifelse(g$arm == 0L, 0L, ifelse(g$time > Tp, g$arm, 0L)) g$y   <- mapply(function(t, i, a)   sum(U[t, ] * Lam[a + 1L, ] * V[i, ]) + 50 + rnorm(1, sd = .3),   g$time, g$id, g$arm)  fit_multi <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = g, method = "si") fit_multi$arm_estimates       # per-arm ATTs #>          1          2  #> -0.2139400  0.1224433 fit_multi$estimate            # weighted aggregate #> [1] -0.04574832

title: Estimators
text: Abadie & Zhao (2026). scm_design() different, design-oriented interface (selects units expose planned intervention). offers "base", "weakly_targeted", "unit_level" variants blank-period permutation tests split-conformal CIs.
code: des <- scm_design(   data = dat, outcome = "y", unit = "id", time = "time",   T0 = T_pre, design = "base" ) des #> === scm_design (Abadie & Zhao 2026) === #> Design variant : base  #> Treated units  : u3  #> ATT estimate   : 0.0063  #> p-value        : NA (no blank periods)

title: Inference
text: Abadie et al. (2010). mspe_ratio_pval() compares treated unit’s post/pre MSPE ratio placebo ratios every donor. returns plain list.
code: mspe <- mspe_ratio_pval(fit_scm, alternative = "two.sided") c(p_value = mspe$p_value, ratio_obs = mspe$ratio_obs) #>    p_value  #> 0.08333333

title: Inference
text: Arkhangelsky et al. (2021); Clarke et al. (2023). sdid_inference() supports "placebo", "bootstrap", "jackknife", "jackknife_global". result broom-friendly. tidy() returns one-row data frame estimate, std.error, p.value, CI — ready rbind() results table. glance() gives compact summary:
code: inf_plac <- sdid_inference(fit_sdid, method = "placebo") inf_boot <- sdid_inference(fit_sdid, method = "bootstrap", n_boot = 100, seed = 1)  tidy(inf_plac) #>   term estimate std.error statistic    p.value conf.low conf.high  method #> 1  ATT 1.821896        NA        NA 0.08333333       NA        NA placebo #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided         11     FALSE tidy(inf_boot) #>   term estimate std.error statistic      p.value conf.low conf.high    method #> 1  ATT 1.821896 0.1287863  14.14666 1.958361e-45 1.600463  2.027934 bootstrap #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided         11     FALSE glance(inf_boot) #>      method n_controls staggered estimate std.error      p.value conf.low #> 1 bootstrap         11     FALSE 1.821896 0.1287863 1.958361e-45 1.600463 #>   conf.high alternative n_boot_valid #> 1  2.027934   two.sided          100

title: Inference
text: Xu (2017). gsc_boot() parametric bootstrap H0 (sharp fits ); gsc_inference() offers non-parametric bootstrap / jackknife.
code: gb <- gsc_boot(fit_gsc, B = 100, alpha = 0.05) c(ci_lower = gb$ci_lower, ci_upper = gb$ci_upper, p_value = gb$p_value) #>   ci_lower   ci_upper    p_value  #> -0.3934672  0.2557330  0.0000000  tidy(gsc_inference(fit_gsc, method = "jackknife")) #>   term estimate  std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high    method #> 1  ATT 1.966522 0.04177117  47.07845       0 1.884652  2.048392 jackknife #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided         11     FALSE

title: Inference
text: Agarwal et al. (2025). si_inference() mirrors GSC non-parametric API also handles staggered multi-arm fits.
code: tidy(si_inference(fit_si, method = "bootstrap", n_boot = 100, seed = 1)) #>   term estimate  std.error statistic      p.value conf.low conf.high    method #> 1  ATT 2.009438 0.05357169  37.50932 6.49154e-308 1.881553  2.080903 bootstrap #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided         11     FALSE

title: Inference
text: Chernozhukov, Wüthrich & Zhu (2021). conformal_inference() works across scm / sdid / gsc / mc / si sharp fits. H0: τ = τ0 re-imputes treated post-period Y1 - τ0, re-estimates counterfactual T periods, inverts moving-block permutation test residuals get p-value confidence interval. p-value tests sharp null τ0 = 0; CI obtained test inversion grid. permutation uses |Π| = T cyclic shifts, smallest attainable p-value 1/T.
code: conf <- conformal_inference(fit_scm, tau0 = 0, level = 0.95) tidy(conf) #>   term estimate std.error statistic p.value   conf.low conf.high    method #> 1  ATT 1.698424        NA        NA     0.1 -0.1077258  3.162582 conformal #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided         11     FALSE

title: Inference
text: Since every inference object tidies columns, comparing methods single rbind():
code: do.call(rbind, list(   cbind(method = "sdid (placebo)",   tidy(inf_plac)[c("estimate","p.value","conf.low","conf.high")]),   cbind(method = "sdid (bootstrap)", tidy(inf_boot)[c("estimate","p.value","conf.low","conf.high")]),   cbind(method = "scm (conformal)",  tidy(conf)[c("estimate","p.value","conf.low","conf.high")]) )) #>             method estimate      p.value   conf.low conf.high #> 1   sdid (placebo) 1.821896 8.333333e-02         NA        NA #> 2 sdid (bootstrap) 1.821896 1.958361e-45  1.6004630  2.027934 #> 3  scm (conformal) 1.698424 1.000000e-01 -0.1077258  3.162582

title: Staggered adoption
text: u1 treated period 11 u2 period 16.
code: set.seed(42) N <- 12; TT <- 20 f   <- cumsum(rnorm(TT, 0, 0.5)) lam <- rnorm(N, 1, 0.3) dat <- expand.grid(time = seq_len(TT), id = paste0("u", seq_len(N))) dat$y <- as.vector(outer(f, lam)) + rnorm(nrow(dat), 0, 0.3)  dat$d <- 0L dat$d[dat$id == "u1" & dat$time > 10] <- 1L dat$d[dat$id == "u2" & dat$time > 15] <- 1L dat$y[dat$d == 1] <- dat$y[dat$d == 1] + 2.0   # true ATT = 2.0

title: Staggered adoption
text: Per-cohort detail cohort_estimates:
code: fit <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "sdid") fit$estimate          # aggregate ATT #> [1] 1.872835 fit$staggered         # TRUE #> [1] TRUE fit$cohort_estimates #>   cohort n_treated T_pre T_post estimate    weight #> 1     11         1    10     10 1.839381 0.6666667 #> 2     16         1    15      5 1.939744 0.3333333

title: Staggered adoption
text: control_group controls units serve donors cohort: "clean" (default) — never-treated units plus -yet-treated units (adopting later current cohort). "never_treated" — never-treated units .
code: fit_clean <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "sdid",                      control_group = "clean") fit_nt    <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = "sdid",                      control_group = "never_treated") c(clean = fit_clean$estimate, never_treated = fit_nt$estimate) #>         clean never_treated  #>      1.872835      1.962071

title: Staggered adoption
text: call works every method:
code: methods <- c("scm", "sdid", "gsc", "mc", "tasc", "si") sapply(methods, function(m)   scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = m)$estimate) #>      scm     sdid      gsc       mc     tasc       si  #> 1.835229 1.872835 1.760712 2.292777 3.153267 1.934303

title: Staggered adoption
text: sdid_inference(), gsc_inference(), si_inference() extend staggered fits. jackknife_global staggered-specific variant removes unique control unit across cohorts , correctly capturing cross-cohort correlation.
code: library(broom) tidy(sdid_inference(fit, method = "bootstrap", n_boot = 100, seed = 1)) #>   term estimate  std.error statistic      p.value conf.low conf.high    method #> 1  ATT 1.872835 0.09273774  20.19496 1.084054e-90 1.711155  2.060448 bootstrap #>   alternative n_controls staggered #> 1   two.sided       10.5      TRUE tidy(sdid_inference(fit, method = "jackknife_global")) #>   term estimate std.error statistic      p.value conf.low conf.high #> 1  ATT 1.872835 0.1186042  15.79063 3.609468e-56 1.640375  2.105295 #>             method alternative n_controls staggered #> 1 jackknife_global   two.sided         11      TRUE

title: Staggered adoption
text: plot() augment() staggered fits operate per cohort; aggregate synthetic series defined (Y_synth = NULL). SCM staggered adoption supports covariates = (partial-) predictors = pred(...) interface. SI additionally supports staggered multi-arm simultaneously — see Estimators.

what: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
text: coresynth high-performance R package provides six causal inference methods panel data unified formula interface. core optimizations (QP solving, SVD, Kalman filtering) implemented C++ via RcppArmadillo, achieving ~56x speedups pure-R implementations typical problem sizes (see Performance section).

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
code: # From GitHub (requires Rtools on Windows, Xcode on macOS) pak::pak("yo5uke/coresynth")  # Via devtools devtools::install_github("yo5uke/coresynth")

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
code: library(coresynth)  # Generate a balanced panel (10 units, 20 periods, true ATT = 2.0) set.seed(42) N <- 10; TT <- 20; T_pre <- 10 f   <- cumsum(rnorm(TT, 0, 0.5)) lam <- rnorm(N, 1, 0.3) dat <- expand.grid(time = seq_len(TT), id = paste0("u", seq_len(N))) dat$y <- as.vector(outer(f, lam)) + rnorm(nrow(dat), 0, 0.3) dat$d <- as.integer(dat$id == "u1" & dat$time > T_pre) dat$y[dat$d == 1] <- dat$y[dat$d == 1] + 2.0   # true ATT = 2  # Run all six methods methods <- c("scm", "sdid", "gsc", "mc", "tasc", "si") fits    <- lapply(methods, \(m) scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat, method = m)) names(fits) <- methods  # Compare ATT estimates (true value = 2.0) data.frame(   method   = methods,   estimate = round(sapply(fits, `[[`, "estimate"), 3) ) #>      method estimate #> scm     scm    2.271 #> sdid   sdid    2.150 #> gsc     gsc    2.255 #> mc       mc    2.696 #> tasc   tasc    1.154 #> si       si    2.346 # SDID trend plot (observed vs. synthetic) plot(fits$sdid, type = "trend") # SCM gap plot (treatment effect over time) plot(fits$scm, type = "gap") # SCM donor weights plot(fits$scm, type = "weights")

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
text: conformal_inference() (Chernozhukov, Wüthrich & Zhu 2021) provides permutation-based p-values confidence intervals sharp fits across scm/sdid/gsc/mc/si.

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
text: six methods support staggered adoption using cohort-based approach (Clarke et al. 2023): adoption cohort fitted separately cohort ATTs aggregated weights proportional N_treated × T_post.
code: # u1: treated from t=11, u2: treated from t=16 dat_s        <- dat dat_s$d      <- 0L dat_s$d[dat_s$id == "u1" & dat_s$time > 10] <- 1L dat_s$d[dat_s$id == "u2" & dat_s$time > 15] <- 1L dat_s$y[dat_s$d == 1] <- dat_s$y[dat_s$d == 1] + 2.0  # All methods detect staggered timing automatically fit_sdid <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat_s, method = "sdid") fit_gsc  <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat_s, method = "gsc") fit_mc   <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat_s, method = "mc") fit_si   <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat_s, method = "si")  # Cohort-level estimates are accessible fit_sdid$cohort_estimates #>   cohort estimate weight n_treated T_pre T_post #> 1     11    1.97  0.667         1    10      9 #> 2     16    2.03  0.333         1    10      4  # control_group = "clean" (default) uses never-treated + future-adopters as donors # control_group = "never_treated" restricts to never-treated only fit_sdid_clean <- scm_fit(y ~ d | id + time, data = dat_s, method = "sdid",                           control_group = "never_treated")

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
text: SCM supports covariate-based matching following Abadie et al. (2010) §2.3. Use pred(vars, times, op) specify variables time windows include predictor matrix: pred() call aggregates one variables time window using op = "mean" (default), "median", "sum". Multiple pred() calls different windows can combined freely list.
code: # Assume dat has extra columns: income, unemp fit_scm_cov <- scm_fit(   y ~ d | id + time,   data   = dat,   method = "scm",   predictors = list(     pred(c("income", "unemp"), 1:8),   # average income & unemp over pre-period     pred("y", 5),                       # outcome at a specific pre-treatment year     pred("y", 1:4, op = "mean")         # outcome averaged over early pre-period   ) ) summary(fit_scm_cov)   # shows predictor balance table

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
text: GSC supports time-varying covariate adjustment via full EM algorithm Xu (2017). Pass character vector column names covariates: EM loop alternates : E-step: SVD Ỹ=Yit−xit′β̂\tilde{Y}_{} = Y_{} - x_{}'\hat\beta update factors F̂\hat{F}, Λ̂\hat\Lambda M-step: Ridge OLS residuals xitx_{} update β̂\hat\beta Treated unit loadings estimated covariate-demeaned pre-treatment data per Xu (2017) Step 2. covariates = NULL (default), plain 3-step SVD estimator (β̂=0\hat\beta = 0) used.
code: # Assume dat has a time-varying column: gdp_growth fit_gsc_cov <- scm_fit(   y ~ d | id + time,   data       = dat,   method     = "gsc",   r          = 2,   covariates = "gdp_growth" ) fit_gsc_cov$beta   # estimated beta coefficient(s)

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
code: # SCM: MSPE ratio placebo test (Abadie et al. 2010) scm_p <- mspe_ratio_pval(fits$scm) cat("p-value:", scm_p$p_value, "\n")  # SDID: four inference methods — placebo, bootstrap, jackknife, jackknife_global sdid_inf <- sdid_inference(fits$sdid, method = "placebo") tidy(sdid_inf)   # broom-style one-row data.frame  sdid_boot <- sdid_inference(fits$sdid, method = "bootstrap", n_boot = 200, seed = 1) tidy(sdid_boot)  # GSC: parametric bootstrap under H0 (sharp only) gsc_ci <- gsc_boot(fits$gsc, B = 200, alpha = 0.05) cat("95% CI: [", gsc_ci$ci_lower, ",", gsc_ci$ci_upper, "]\n")  # GSC / SI: non-parametric inference (sharp + staggered) gsc_inf <- gsc_inference(fits$gsc, method = "jackknife") si_inf  <- si_inference(fits$si,  method = "bootstrap", n_boot = 200, seed = 1) tidy(gsc_inf) tidy(si_inf)  # Conformal inference (Chernozhukov, Wüthrich & Zhu 2021) — sharp fits for # scm / sdid / gsc / mc / si. Re-estimates the counterfactual under the null # and inverts a moving-block permutation test for a p-value and CI. conf <- conformal_inference(fits$scm, tau0 = 0, level = 0.95) tidy(conf)

title: Fast and Unified Synthetic Control Methods
code: library(broom)  # Extract weights as a data frame tidy(fits$scm)  # Summary row glance(fits$scm)  # JSON export (for reproducibility and AI workflows) export_json(fits$scm, file = "scm_result.json")

what: Augmented Synthetic Control Method (Ridge ASCM) — augment_scm
title: Augmented Synthetic Control Method (Ridge ASCM) — augment_scm
text: Applies ridge-regression-based bias correction fitted SCM object, following Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein (2021, JASA). corrected estimator :

what: Conformal Inference for Synthetic Control Estimators — conformal_inference
title: Conformal Inference for Synthetic Control Estimators — conformal_inference
text: Implements permutation-based conformal inference procedure Chernozhukov, Wuthrich & Zhu (2021, JASA). test inverts sharp null \(H_0: \tau = \tau_0\) imputing treated post-treatment counterfactual \(Y_{1t} - \tau_0\), re-estimating counterfactual proxy \(T\) periods (imposing null), computing moving-block permutation p-value estimated residuals. confidence interval obtained test inversion grid candidate \(\tau_0\).

title: Conformal Inference for Synthetic Control Estimators — conformal_inference
code: conformal_inference(   fit,   tau0 = 0,   q = 1,   alternative = c("two.sided", "greater", "less"),   ci = TRUE,   level = 0.95,   grid = NULL,   n_grid = 200L,   grid_mult = 4,   ... )

title: Conformal Inference for Synthetic Control Estimators — conformal_inference
text: fit coresynth object scm_fit(). tau0 Null value ATT reported p-value (default 0). q Exponent \(S_q\) test statistic (S_q = (T_post^{-1} \sum |u_t|^q)^{1/q}). Default 1, robust heavy-tailed data (CWZ 2021). Used alternative = "two.sided"; one-sided tests use signed mean post-treatment residual. alternative "two.sided" (default), "greater", "less". ci Logical; construct confidence interval test inversion (default TRUE). level Confidence level interval (default 0.95). grid Optional numeric vector candidate \(\tau_0\) values test inversion. NULL (default), grid n_grid points centred point estimate half-width grid_mult times pre-treatment residual standard deviation. n_grid Number grid points grid = NULL (default 200). grid_mult Half-width multiplier grid = NULL (default 4). ... Unused.

text: data Long-format data frame (one row per unit–time). outcome Name outcome column. unit Name unit identifier column. time Name time identifier column. T0 Last pre-experimental period (value present time column).  Periods T0 experimental periods. T_fit Number fitting periods, counted start pre-experimental phase.  Defaults NULL, uses pre-experimental periods fitting (blank periods; inference disabled).  T_fit smaller total number pre-experimental periods, remaining periods become blank periods used inference. m_min Minimum number units assigned treatment (default 1). m_max Maximum number units assigned treatment (default 1). f Named numeric vector population weights \(f_j\). Defaults uniform weights \(1/J\).  normalised sum 1. predictors list() pred() specifications define predictor matrix \(X_j\).  Defaults NULL, uses fitting-period outcome values predictors. design Design formulation: "base" (default), "weakly_targeted", "unit_level". beta Trade-parameter \(\beta > 0\) Weakly targeted design (default 1). xi Trade-parameter \(\xi > 0\) Unit-level design (default 1). alpha Significance level confidence intervals (default 0.05). normalize TRUE (default), row predictor matrix divided cross-unit standard deviation optimisation, predictors measured different scales contribute equally. max_subsets Maximum number treatment-set candidates evaluate switching random sampling (default 100 000).

title: Experimental Synthetic Control Design — scm_design
text: object class "scm_design" components: treated_units: unit identifiers selected treatment control_units: unit identifiers control pool w: J-length weight vector synthetic treated unit (sums 1) v: J-length weight vector synthetic control unit (sums 1) tau_hat: estimated treatment effects experimental period p_value: permutation p-value (NA blank periods unavailable) ci_lower, ci_upper: per-period split-conformal confidence interval Y_synth_tr, Y_synth_co: synthetic treated/control series (periods) estimate: ATT (mean tau_hat)

title: Experimental Synthetic Control Design — scm_design
text: Three design formulations available: "base" (eq. 7): synthetic treated synthetic control independently target population average \(\bar{X}\). "weakly_targeted" (eq. 9): synthetic treated targets \(\bar{X}\); synthetic control targets synthetic treated predictor vector (controlled beta). "unit_level" (eq. 10): treated unit gets synthetic control; aggregate control weight convex combination (controlled xi). Inference uses "blank periods" — pre-experimental periods whose outcomes used estimate weights.  Set T_fit strictly smaller number pre-experimental periods enable permutation test split- conformal confidence intervals Section 3 Abadie Zhao (2026).

title: Experimental Synthetic Control Design — scm_design

title: Fit a Synthetic Control Method Model — scm_fit
text: formula Formula object, e.g. y ~ D | unit + time. data data.frame long format (one row per unit-time). method One "scm", "sdid", "gsc", "mc", "tasc", "si". predictors list() pred() specifications define predictor matrix SCM (see Abadie et al. 2010, S.2.3). pred() entry aggregates one variables time window. Pass NULL (default) use pre-treatment outcome periods predictors. Applies method = "scm" . covariates optional named list additional time-varying covariates partial estimation. element character string naming column data. Supported method = "sdid", "scm", "gsc". v_selection V matrix selection method method = "scm". "insample" (default) follows Abadie et al. (2010): V chosen minimising -sample pre-treatment MSPE. "oos" follows Abadie (2021) S.3.2: pre-treatment window split half; V selected minimise MSPE validation half, W refit full window. donor_mspe_threshold Donor pool filtering threshold (Abadie 2021 S.4). method = "scm" . donor's individual pre-treatment MSPE (using donor alone counterfactual) divided minimum MSPE across donors. Donors whose ratio exceeds threshold excluded estimation. Inf (default) disables filtering. lambda_pen Penalised SCM parameter (Abadie & L'Hour 2021, JASA). method = "scm" . NULL (default) runs standard unpenalised SCM. "auto" selects penalty via --sample pre-treatment MSPE validation window v_selection = "oos". non-negative number uses value directly. v_optim Outer V-optimisation method method = "scm". "coord_descent" (default) uses existing C++ coordinate descent 11-point grid search – fastest k = T_pre large (outcomes-). "bfgs" uses R's L-BFGS-B, requires O(k^2) inner QP calls faster k small (e.g. external predictors k <= 15). "auto" selects "bfgs" k <= 15, otherwise "coord_descent". ... Additional arguments forwarded specific method (e.g. r, lambda, zeta2).

title: coresynth 0.2.0
text: Conformal inference (conformal_inference()): permutation-based p-values confidence intervals following Chernozhukov, Wüthrich & Zhu (2021). Works sharp fits across supported estimation methods (scm, sdid, gsc, mc, si). counterfactual proxy re-estimated null T periods (essential finite-sample validity per CWZ §2.2), p-values obtained via moving-block (cyclic-shift) permutation estimated residuals. Confidence intervals constructed test inversion user-supplied automatically chosen grid. Returns coresynth_inference subclass compatible tidy() glance().

title: coresynth 0.2.0
text: panel_to_matrices(): fill loop replaced vectorised match() + matrix-index assignment; removes O(n × (T + N)) bottleneck shared data-prep path. tasc.cpp: safe_inv_sympd() helper added Kalman filter degrades pinv instead aborting innovation covariance numerically PD. %||% null-coalescing helper centralised utils.R; duplicate definitions broom.R plot.R removed. check_sharp_adoption() (unused internal function) removed.

title: coresynth 0.1.0
text: First public release.

title: coresynth 0.1.0
text: SCM (Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller 2010): Synthetic Control Method unified formula interface. Supports predictor variables via pred(), --sample V selection (v_selection = "oos"), donor filtering (donor_mspe_threshold), penalised SCM (lambda_pen), staggered adoption. Inference: MSPE ratio permutation test via mspe_ratio_pval(). SDID (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021): Synthetic Difference--Differences. Supports time-varying covariates (covariates =), sharp staggered adoption. Inference: sdid_inference() placebo / bootstrap / jackknife / jackknife_global. GSC (Xu 2017): Generalised Synthetic Control interactive fixed effects. Supports time-varying covariates via full EM algorithm, sharp staggered adoption. Inference: parametric bootstrap (gsc_boot()) non-parametric (gsc_inference()). MC (Athey et al. 2021): Matrix Completion via nuclear-norm regularisation (Soft-Impute). Supports sharp staggered adoption. TASC (Rho et al. 2026): Time-Aware Synthetic Control via Kalman EM. Supports sharp staggered adoption. SI (Agarwal et al. 2025): Synthetic Interventions via SI-PCR. Supports sharp, staggered, multi-arm (K > 1), staggered × multi-arm. Inference: si_inference() bootstrap / jackknife / jackknife_global. SCM-Design (Abadie & Zhao 2026): scm_design() base / weakly_targeted / unit_level variants, blank-period permutation test, split-conformal confidence intervals.

title: coresynth 0.1.0
text: Single scm_fit(outcome ~ treatment | unit + time, data, method = ...) entry point methods. panel_to_tensor() multi-arm SI data preparation. broom integration: tidy(), glance(), augment() methods inference objects. plot.coresynth(): trend, gap, weights plots via ggplot2. export_json(): JSON export reproducibility.
